301: Chilean general and dictator who led the coup against Allende in 1973 and ruled with an iron fist until 1990.
302: First Prime Minister of India after independence, a close ally of Gandhi and a key figure of decolonization.
303: Egyptian president who nationalized the Suez Canal in 1956 and championed pan-Arabism, becoming an icon of 20th-century Arab nationalism.
304: American novelist and 1962 Nobel Prize in Literature laureate, author of The Grapes of Wrath and Of Mice and Men.
305: American novelist and 1949 Nobel laureate, a master of stream-of-consciousness prose and author of The Sound and the Fury.
306: French novelist and author of In Search of Lost Time, one of the landmark works of 20th-century literature.
307: Führer of Germany from 1934 until his death in 1945, he brought the National Socialist German Workers' Party to power and established a totalitarian regime.
308: Soviet leader who shaped official Communist Party doctrine as Marxism-Leninism; his own version of it became known as Stalinism.
309: 26th President of the United States, champion of progressivism, national parks, and the 'Big Stick' foreign policy.
310: 28th President of the United States, who led the country through World War I and promoted the League of Nations.
311: 33rd President of the United States, who ordered the atomic bombing of Japan and designed the containment doctrine of the Cold War.
312: 36th President of the United States, who signed the Civil Rights Act of 1964 and escalated the Vietnam War.
313: 37th President of the United States, who opened diplomatic relations with China and resigned following the Watergate scandal.
314: 39th President of the United States and Nobel Peace Prize laureate, recognized for his humanitarian work after his presidency.
315: 41st President of the United States, who oversaw the end of the Cold War and led the Gulf War coalition.
316: 42nd President of the United States, who presided over a period of economic prosperity in the 1990s.
317: 43rd President of the United States, who responded to the September 11 attacks by launching the wars in Afghanistan and Iraq.
318: 44th President of the United States, the first African American to hold the office and recipient of the Nobel Peace Prize in 2009.
319: 45th and 47th President of the United States, a businessman and media personality who became the standard-bearer of conservative populism.
320: 46th President of the United States, with a long career in the Senate and as Vice President under Obama.
321: U.S. Secretary of State and the first woman nominated for the presidency by a major American political party.
322: U.S. Secretary of State and National Security Advisor, architect of détente with the USSR and the opening to China.
323: First Chancellor of the Federal Republic of Germany and architect of post-war reconstruction.
324: German Chancellor, champion of the Ostpolitik policy of engagement with Eastern Europe, and 1971 Nobel Peace Prize laureate.
325: German Chancellor who led the reunification of Germany in 1990.
326: First female Chancellor of Germany (2005–2021) and a central figure in European politics for 16 years.
327: Socialist President of France (1981–1995) and a key figure in European integration.
328: President of France (1995–2007) who formally acknowledged French responsibility in the Holocaust.
329: President of France since 2017 and founder of the centrist movement La République En Marche.
330: British Prime Minister (1997–2007), leader of New Labour, and a key ally in the Iraq War.
331: Media mogul and three-time Prime Minister of Italy, a controversial figure in Italian politics.
332: First President of the Russian Federation after the dissolution of the USSR.
333: President of Russia and the dominant figure in its politics since 2000.
334: President of Ukraine since 2019 who led the country during the Russian invasion that began in 2022.
335: Leader of socialist Yugoslavia and a key figure in the Non-Aligned Movement.
336: Leader of the Solidarity trade union and first post-communist president of Poland, 1983 Nobel Peace Prize laureate.
337: Playwright and first president of post-communist Czechoslovakia and later the Czech Republic.
338: Historic leader of the PLO and president of the Palestinian National Authority, 1994 Nobel Peace Prize laureate.
339: Israeli Prime Minister who signed the Oslo Accords and was assassinated by an extremist in 1995.
340: Egyptian president who signed the Camp David peace accord with Israel and received the Nobel Peace Prize in 1978.
341: First female Prime Minister of Israel, who led the country during the Yom Kippur War.
342: Founder of the State of Israel and its first Prime Minister.
343: Israel's longest-serving Prime Minister and leader of the Likud party.
344: Iraqi president who waged wars against Iran and Kuwait and was overthrown in the 2003 invasion.
345: Ayatollah who led the Islamic Revolution of 1979 and founded the Islamic Republic of Iran.
346: Ruler of Libya for 42 years, overthrown and killed during the civil war of 2011.
347: Founding father of republican China after the fall of the Qing dynasty.
348: Chinese Nationalist leader of the Kuomintang and president of the Republic of China on Taiwan after 1949.
349: Premier of the People's Republic of China under Mao and the architect of its foreign policy.
350: Chinese leader who launched the economic reforms that transformed China into a global power.
351: General Secretary of the Chinese Communist Party and President of the People's Republic of China since 2013.
352: Vietnamese revolutionary leader and founder of the Democratic Republic of Vietnam.
353: Leader of the Khmer Rouge and architect of the Cambodian genocide (1975–1979).
354: First president of Indonesia and leader of its independence movement against the Netherlands.
355: Founding Prime Minister of Singapore and the architect of its transformation into an economic powerhouse.
356: Founder and lifelong leader of North Korea following the Korean War.
357: Supreme Leader of North Korea since 2011 and the third generation of the Kim dynasty.
358: Founder of Pakistan and leader of the Muslim League during the partition of India.
359: First female Prime Minister of India, assassinated by her own bodyguards in 1984.
360: First female Prime Minister of a Muslim-majority country, assassinated in a 2007 attack.
361: Leader of Ghanaian independence and a central figure of Pan-Africanism.
362: First Prime Minister of independent Congo, assassinated in an operation with foreign backing.
363: Last Emperor of Ethiopia, a symbol of resistance against fascism and a revered figure in Rastafarianism.
364: Leader of Zimbabwean independence who became an authoritarian president for nearly four decades.
365: Three-time president of Argentina and founder of Peronism.
366: President of Venezuela and promoter of '21st-century socialism' across Latin America.
367: Trade union leader and president of Brazil across three terms, a leading figure of the Latin American left.
368: American chemist and the only person to win two unshared Nobel Prizes, in Chemistry and Peace.
369: American physicist, Nobel laureate for quantum electrodynamics and a legendary science communicator.
370: British physicist who predicted the existence of antimatter and formulated the equation bearing his name.
371: Austrian physicist who formulated the exclusion principle governing the behavior of electrons.
372: Physicist who co-discovered nuclear fission but was overlooked for the Nobel Prize awarded to Otto Hahn.
373: Astronomer who demonstrated the expansion of the universe and the existence of galaxies beyond the Milky Way.
374: Italian physicist who built the first nuclear reactor and participated in the Manhattan Project.
375: Molecular biologist and co-discoverer of the double-helix structure of DNA.
376: Molecular biologist who, together with Watson, co-discovered the structure of DNA.
377: Chemist whose X-ray diffraction images were crucial to discovering the structure of DNA.
378: Virologist who developed the first effective vaccine against poliomyelitis.
379: Agronomist and father of the Green Revolution, awarded the Nobel Peace Prize for saving millions from famine.
380: Marine biologist whose book Silent Spring sparked the modern environmental movement.
381: British primatologist and pioneer in the study of chimpanzees in their natural habitat.
382: Soviet physicist and father of the hydrogen bomb who became a dissident and Nobel Peace Prize laureate for his defense of human rights.
383: Hungarian-American mathematician, pioneer of game theory and the architecture of modern computers.
384: Father of information theory, the mathematical foundation of digital communication.
385: Computing pioneer who developed the first compiler and the COBOL programming language.
386: Software engineer who led the development of the flight software for the Apollo program.
387: NASA mathematician whose calculations made the Mercury and Apollo missions possible.
388: Creator of the C programming language and co-creator of the Unix operating system.
389: Inventor of the World Wide Web and the HTTP and HTML protocols.
390: Creator of the Linux kernel and the Git version control system.
391: Co-founder of Microsoft and philanthropist, one of the wealthiest people in the world.
392: Co-founder of Apple and designer of the Apple I and Apple II computers.
393: Founder of Amazon and the aerospace company Blue Origin.
394: Entrepreneur and founder of Tesla and SpaceX, a disruptive force in the technology industry of the 21st century.
395: Co-founder of Google and co-author of the PageRank algorithm.
396: Co-founder of Google alongside Larry Page.
398: German-American political philosopher and author of The Origins of Totalitarianism.
399: Philosopher of language and author of the Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus and Philosophical Investigations.
400: Philosopher, mathematician, and pacifist activist, awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1950.
